elements.label: 1[i]

elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner argues that Feldman 'discloses that the transduced signal is correlated to an amount or concentration of analyte' and 'further discloses a graph of electrical current versus glucose concentration for electrochemical sensors.' Dr. Smith testifies that 'a POSITA would have understood that, in the case of an electrochemical glucose sensor as disclosed in Feldman, the working electrode is configured to measure the electrical current that is indicative of a glucose concentration.' Based on the complete record before us and for the reasons expressed above, we are persuaded that Petitioner has shown a preponderance of evidence that claim 1 would have been obvious over Feldman.

elements.label: [26.pre]
elements.text: A tire pressure detecting system, comprising: an identification rewritable tire pressure detector comprising: a micro-processing module having a rewritable memory unit to record an identification; a sensing module electrically connected to the micro-processing module and having a pressure-detecting unit to detect a tire pressure and send a detection result to the micro-processing module; a transmitting module controlled by the micro-processing module to transmit a radio frequency (RF) signal, wherein the RF signal comprises the detection result and the identification of the identification rewritable tire pressure detector; a power module electronically connected to the micro-processing module to supply power to the identification rewriteable tire pressure detector; and an interface arranged to receive an external signal and send the external signal to the micro-processing module, wherein the external signal comprises an external identification to be written into the rewriteable memory unit or to be used to overwrite a preset identification in the rewriteable memory unit;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not individually analyze the tire pressure detector limitations of claim 26. The Board focused on two dispositive issues: (1) the "stores only the old identification" limitation and (2) whether Tang's manufacturers' codes disclose the claimed detector ID. elements.label: [26.a]
elements.text: a portable setting apparatus arranged to communicate with the identification rewriteable tire pressure detector, comprising: a control module; an input module connected to the control module to enable an operator to manually input an identification to be written into the identification rewriteable tire pressure detector; a receiving module connected to the control module to receive the RF signal from the identification rewriteable tire pressure detector or a tire pressure detector and to send the RF signal to the control module; a setting output module controlled by the control module to send the external signal to the interface of the identification rewriteable tire pressure detector, wherein the external signal is generated by the control module and comprises the identification that is provided by the input module or provided by the RF signal received from the receiving module; and a power source connected to the control module to supply power to the setting apparatus;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not individually analyze the setting apparatus structural limitations of claim 26. The Board focused on two dispositive issues: (1) the "stores only the old identification" limitation and (2) whether Tang's manufacturers' codes disclose the claimed detector ID. The Board concluded: "we determine Petitioner has not met its burden to show a reasonable likelihood it would prevail with respect to the contention that claims 26 and 27 are unpatentable over the combination of Tang and Lemense."

elements.label: [26.b]
elements.text: obtain an update identification to be written into a new identification rewriteable tire pressure detector either by (1) receiving the RF signal from an old tire pressure detector by the receiving module, retrieving an old identification of the old tire pressure detector from the RF signal, and using the old identification as the update identification, or by (2) receiving a manual input of the identification from the input module, and using the identification as the update identification;
elements.board_determination_text: We are not persuaded that Tang's manufacturers' codes are, include, or relate to detector IDs. Tang's manufacturers' codes identify the signaling format to be used for transmitting wireless signal 18, and there is no suggestion in Tang that the manufacturers' codes identify a particular detector. At best, Tang's manufacturers' codes identify tire pressure monitoring systems but do not identify particular detectors or sensors within the system.

elements.label: [26.c]
elements.text: wherein the old tire pressure detector stores only the old identification;
elements.board_determination_text: For the above reasons, Petitioner fails to persuade us that the combination of Tang and Lemense discloses the limitation "wherein the old tire pressure detector stores only the old identification." Petitioner does not provide adequate reasoning or evidence to support the assertion or explain sufficiently why one of ordinary skill in the art would have known that tire pressure sensors typically are programmed at the factory with a single ID. Furthermore, even if assuming for the sake of argument that Petitioner's assertion is correct, the Petition fails to explain adequately how or why one of ordinary skill in the art would have modified the combination of Tang and Lemense to store only the old identification in the old tire pressure detector.

elements.label: [1Pre]
elements.text: A bootstrap refresh control circuit for a voltage converter, wherein the voltage converter comprises a high side switch, a low side switch and a bootstrap capacitor for providing a bootstrap voltage signal to supply a high side driver of the high side switch, and wherein the voltage converter is configured to receive an input voltage at an input terminal and to provide an output voltage at an output terminal based on driving the high side switch and the low side switch to switch on and off, and wherein the bootstrap refresh control circuit comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately analyze the preamble. The decision focused on limitation 1D as the dispositive element for denying institution.

elements.label: [1A]
elements.text: a bootstrap refresh module, wherein the bootstrap refresh module comprises a first comparing module having a first input terminal, a second input terminal and an output terminal, and wherein the first input terminal of the first comparing module is configured to receive the bootstrap voltage signal, the second input terminal of the first comparing module is configured to receive a bootstrap refresh threshold, and the first comparing module is configured to compare the bootstrap voltage signal with the bootstrap refresh threshold to provide a first comparing signal having a first logic state and a second logic state at the output terminal, and wherein the first comparing signal has the first logic state when the bootstrap voltage signal is lower than the bootstrap refresh threshold, and wherein the first comparing signal has the second logic state when the bootstrap voltage signal is larger than the bootstrap refresh threshold;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board noted an inconsistent mapping issue between 1A and 1C for the Galinski ground ("Due to this apparent inconsistent mapping, Petitioner has not shown with the requisite particularity how Galinski discloses both limitations 1A and 1C"), but the dispositive finding for both the TI and Galinski grounds was limitation 1D. The Board did not independently find 1A not disclosed.

elements.label: [1B]
elements.text: a voltage difference module having a first input terminal, a second input terminal and an output terminal, wherein the first input terminal of the voltage difference module is configured to receive a feedback signal representing the output voltage of the voltage converter, the second input terminal of the voltage difference module is configured to receive a reference voltage signal representing a desired value of the output voltage of the voltage converter, the voltage difference module is configured to compare the feedback signal with the reference voltage signal so as to provide a difference signal at the output terminal;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board never specifically analyzed whether limitation 1B was disclosed. The decision resolved the TI ground on limitation 1D and the Galinski ground on the inconsistent mapping of 1A/1C plus limitation 1D.

elements.label: [1C]
elements.text: when the first comparing signal has the first logic state, the bootstrap refresh module is configured to decrease the output voltage of the voltage converter;
elements.board_determination_text: For the Galinski ground, the Board found an inconsistent mapping between 1A and 1C ("Due to this apparent inconsistent mapping, Petitioner has not shown with the requisite particularity how Galinski discloses both limitations 1A and 1C"), but the primary dispositive element across all grounds was limitation 1D. The Board resolved the TI ground on 1D alone.

elements.label: [1D]
elements.text: wherein when the feedback signal is smaller than the reference voltage signal, the bootstrap refresh control circuit is configured to control the high side switch and the low side switch to switch on and off based on the difference signal so as to charge the bootstrap capacitor for refreshing the bootstrap voltage signal.
elements.board_determination_text: For the TI ground: "Patent Owner persuasively argues that Petitioner has failed to satisfy its threshold burden regarding limitation 1D, which pertains to the architecture of the bootstrap refresh control circuit." The Board found Petitioner "has not adequately explained how that equates to a disclosure in TI of the recited 'bootstrap refresh control circuit [that] is configured to control the [two switches] . . . based on the difference signal so as to charge the bootstrap capacitor for refreshing the bootstrap voltage signal.'" For the Galinski ground: "Petitioner has not explained adequately the apparent inconsistent mapping of modes to the limitations of claim 1 and has not explained adequately how the circuitry of Galinski's normal mode satisfies all of the aspects of limitation 1D."

(Decision: "Because Petitioner has not sufficiently shown that the proposed combination of prior art would disclose each element of claim 1, ... Petitioner’s challenge ... is not reasonably likely to prevail.")

elements.text: determining attributes of areas in which the points are located, wherein the attributes indicate at least whether the points are located in a same pattern piece of the clothing or different pattern pieces of the clothing
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner has not shown that either reference, alone or in combination, can determine whether two points are on the same pattern piece or different pattern pieces by affirmatively examining "attributes of areas" in which the points are located. (Decision: "Second, Petitioner has not shown that either reference, alone or in combination, can determine whether two points are on the same pattern piece or different pattern pieces by affirmatively examining 'attributes of areas' in which the points are located.")

elements.text: determining a length of a line segment using the points based on the determined attributes of the areas, the determined length of the line segment changed to account for separation between the pattern pieces responsive to determining that the points are located in different pattern pieces
elements.board_determination_text: The proposed combination does not determine the 'length of a line segment using the points based on the determined attributes of the areas' and Petitioner misinterprets the claim language about changing the determined length to account for separation; the claimed subtraction of the portion in the empty space (as in Figure 4) is not shown by the prior-art combination. (Decision: "Third, ... the combination does not determine the 'length of a line segment using the points based on the determined attributes of the areas'... Fourth, Petitioner misinterprets the claim language 'the determined length of the line segment changed to account for separation between the pattern pieces' ... It means that the portion of the line segment that is in the empty space between the pattern pieces is subtracted, as shown in Figure 4 of the '448 Patent.")

elements.text: outputting the length of the line segment
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately analyze this "outputting" limitation; instead, it concluded Petitioner had not shown the proposed combination disclosed each element of claim 1, addressing the primary deficiencies summarized above. (Decision: "Because Petitioner has not sufficiently shown that the proposed combination of prior art would disclose each element of claim 1, ... Petitioner’s challenge ... is not reasonably likely to prevail.")

elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately construe or make an explicit finding as to the preamble. The decision focuses its institution analysis on the claim body limitations, particularly [1D], rather than addressing the preamble independently.

elements.label: [1A]
elements.text: a bootstrap refresh module, wherein the bootstrap refresh module comprises a first comparing module having a first input terminal, a second input terminal and an output terminal, and wherein the first input terminal of the first comparing module is configured to receive the bootstrap voltage signal, the second input terminal of the first comparing module is configured to receive a bootstrap refresh threshold, and the first comparing module is configured to compare the bootstrap voltage signal with the bootstrap refresh threshold to provide a first comparing signal having a first logic state and a second logic state at the output terminal, and wherein the first comparing signal has the first logic state when the bootstrap voltage signal is lower than the bootstrap refresh threshold, and wherein the first comparing signal has the second logic state when the bootstrap voltage signal is larger than the bootstrap refresh threshold;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make an individual determination on [1A]. For Ground 1 (TI), the Board focused on [1D] as the dispositive limitation and denied institution on that basis without separately ruling on [1A]. For Ground 2 (Galinski), the Board found Petitioner's overall mapping inconsistent across limitations but did not make a per-element finding on [1A] specifically.

elements.label: [1B]
elements.text: a voltage difference module having a first input terminal, a second input terminal and an output terminal, wherein the first input terminal of the voltage difference module is configured to receive a feedback signal representing the output voltage of the voltage converter, the second input terminal of the voltage difference module is configured to receive a reference voltage signal representing a desired value of the output voltage of the voltage converter, the voltage difference module is configured to compare the feedback signal with the reference voltage signal so as to provide a difference signal at the output terminal;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make an individual determination on [1B]. For Ground 1 (TI), the Board focused on [1D] as the dispositive limitation and denied institution on that basis without separately ruling on [1B]. Patent Owner did not even challenge [1B] in the TI ground. For Ground 2 (Galinski), the Board noted Petitioner's inconsistent mapping of different modes to different limitations but did not make a per-element finding on [1B] specifically.

elements.label: [1C]
elements.text: when the first comparing signal has the first logic state, the bootstrap refresh module is configured to decrease the output voltage of the voltage converter;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make an individual determination on [1C]. For Ground 1 (TI), the Board focused on [1D] as the dispositive limitation and denied institution on that basis without separately ruling on [1C]. For Ground 2 (Galinski), the Board found Petitioner's mapping inconsistent because it relied on different modes of operation for [1A]/[1C] versus [1B]/[1D], but this was an overall mapping deficiency finding rather than a per-element determination that [1C] was or was not disclosed.

elements.label: [1D]
elements.text: wherein when the feedback signal is smaller than the reference voltage signal, the bootstrap refresh control circuit is configured to control the high side switch and the low side switch to switch on and off based on the difference signal so as to charge the bootstrap capacitor for refreshing the bootstrap voltage signal.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board found Petitioner failed to show this limitation was taught by the cited prior art. For Ground 1 (TI), the Board stated: "Petitioner does not adequately explain how all of these aspects are disclosed together by TI such that the reference anticipates claim 1" and concluded "We determine that Petitioner has failed to demonstrate a reasonable likelihood of prevailing in its challenge to claim 1 as anticipated by or as obvious over TI." The Board emphasized that Petitioner's expert opinion on this point was conclusory and lacked citations to TI, and that TI uses adjustable fixed frequency, peak current mode control where an internal oscillator initiates the high-side switch turn-on, which differs from the claimed control based on the difference signal. For Ground 2 (Galinski), the Board found Petitioner relied on Galinski's normal operation mode for [1D] but has not explained adequately how that mode satisfies all aspects of limitation 1D.

elements.label: [1pre]
elements.text: A power supply apparatus, comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: The Board reproduced the preamble as [1pre] in the illustrative claim but the decision does not analyze the preamble separately or reach a distinct conclusion as to the preamble.

elements.label: [1a]
elements.text: a bearing plate;
elements.board_determination_text: We do not find Petitioner's argument and evidence are sufficient to support Zeng-014's disclosure of a bearing plate.

elements.label: [1b]
elements.text: insulation material formed on two opposite surfaces of the bearing plate;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner fails to explain how Park's magnet could be 'formed on two opposite surfaces' of insulating material 123 when insulating substrate 123 and the coil are wrapped around the magnet. Accordingly, we find that Petitioner's assertion that insulating material is the bearing plate is insufficiently supported and, additionally, is inconsistent with Petitioner's assertions for other limitations of the claims.

elements.label: [1c]
elements.text: a plurality of pins electrically connected to the bearing plate and allocated along lateral sides of the insulation material, wherein two terminals of each of the pins have two SMD pads, wherein the two SMD pads of each of the pins are extending to an upper surface and a lower surface of the insulation material, respectively.
elements.board_determination_text: We agree with Patent Owner that Petitioner's case for obviousness is tainted by improper hindsight bias to reach the invention of claims 1 and 10. . . . Thus, we are not persuaded that Petitioner has provided sufficient support for its rationale to combine Vinciarelli-218 with Zeng-014.

elements.label: [1.A]
elements.text: administering to a subject in need thereof an effective amount of ganaxolone to suppress SE
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately determine whether this element was disclosed. The Board's analysis focused entirely on the plasma concentration limitation (the "wherein" clause) as the dispositive issue: "we have determined that the \"wherein\" clause of independent claim 1 is limi[t]ing. In other words, the claim requires that the effective amount of ganaxolone is administered as an IV bolus plus continuous infusion and that such administration produces and maintains a ganaxolone plasma concentration of about 500 to 1000 ng/ml for about 8 to 12 hours."

elements.text: wherein the effective amount of ganaxolone is administered as an intravenous bolus plus continuous infusion to produce and maintain a ganaxolone plasma concentration of about 500 ng/ml to about 1000 ng/ml for about 8 hours to about 12 hours

elements.text: A method for increasing data rates and data throughput in a network, the method comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner has not demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of success in proving that any one of the Challenged Claims is unpatentable. (Deciding denial of institution based on petition's failure, principally with respect to the 'partially filling' limitation.)

elements.text: selecting at least a first channel and a second channel, wherein the first channel and the second channel are adjacent without any other channels therebetween, wherein the first channel and the second channel each have a plurality of data subcarriers, wherein the data subcarriers of the first channel and the data subcarriers of the second channel are separated by a frequency gap corresponding to one or more guard bands between the first and second channels;
elements.board_determination_text: We determine that Petitioner has not established a reasonable likelihood that it would prevail in showing that any one of independent claims 1, 16, and 18 is unpatentable as obvious over either of these two combinations. (The Board did not reach a detailed, independent finding as to this specific limitation because the petition failed on the key 'partially filling' limitation.)

elements.text: partially filling the frequency gap between the first channel and the second channel by adding one or more data subcarriers into the frequency gap such that the one or more guard bands are at least partially filled with at least some of the one or more data subcarriers using full spectral synthesis capability of a fast fourier transform or an inverse fast fourier transform;
elements.board_determination_text: We determine that 'partially,' as recited in the claim 1 limitation 'partially filling the frequency gap between the first channel and the second channel by adding one or more data subcarriers into the frequency gap,' excludes 'completely' or 'fully' filling the frequency gap ... we do not find [Petitioner's] arguments ... sufficiently persuasive on the record before us. Rather, we agree with Patent Owner's arguments and cited evidence ... Petitioner failed to meet its burden of proof in this case.

elements.text: combining the first channel and the second channel using channel bonding with orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM);
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner has not demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of success in proving that any one of the Challenged Claims is unpatentable. (The Board's denial rests on Petitioner's failure with respect to the 'partially filling' limitation; the Board did not find it necessary to make an independent disclosure determination for this limitation.)

elements.text: transmitting data subcarriers occupying the first channel, the second channel, and the frequency gap in parallel to a receiver.
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner has not demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of success in proving that any one of the Challenged Claims is unpatentable. (The Board denied institution based on the record and Petitioner's failure to show the critical 'partially filling' limitation was met by the prior art.)

elements.label: [1.PRE]
elements.text: A portable activity monitoring device to calculate activity points corresponding to physical activities of a user, the portable activity monitoring device comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: For all of the foregoing reasons, we are persuaded that Petitioner has established by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 1, 10, and 25 are unpatentable under § 102 as anticipated by Kroll.

elements.label: [1.A]
elements.text: a housing having a physical size and shape that is adapted to couple to the body of the user;
elements.board_determination_text: For all of the foregoing reasons, we are persuaded that Petitioner has established by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 1, 10, and 25 are unpatentable under § 102 as anticipated by Kroll.

elements.label: [1.B]
elements.text: a plurality of sensors, disposed in the housing, to generate sensor data which is representative of activity of the user, wherein the plurality of sensors includes at least three accelerometers;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board stated that 'these are not separate and distinct limitations, but rather the "include" language adds specificity to the "plurality of sensors."' The Board was 'persuaded that Kroll's disclosure of a tri-axial accelerometer is sufficient to disclose the recited three accelerometers.' The Board concluded: 'we see no error in Petitioner directing us to a tri-axial accelerometer that includes three accelerometers. For all of the foregoing reasons, we determine that Kroll discloses this limitation.'

elements.label: [1.C]
elements.text: processing circuitry, disposed in the housing and electrically coupled to the plurality of sensors, to:
elements.board_determination_text: We continue to understand Kroll to be providing a singular teaching of a CPU that is a basic building block of Kroll's device and that Kroll did not vary its CPU in its discussion of embodiments and alternative designs. Therefore, there is no issue of mixing embodiments because the same CPU is found in every embodiment of Kroll. Petitioner relies upon Kroll's CPU to disclose the recited 'processing circuitry.'

elements.label: [1.D]
elements.text: calculate the activity points of the user using the sensor data, wherein the activity points correlate to an amount of one or more physical activities of the user;
elements.board_determination_text: We are persuaded by Petitioner's assertions. Kroll calculates and displays 'total expended calories and activity intensities.' Kroll states that the 'composite acceleration' is 'proportional to the calorie expenditure of the user.' Dr. Young persuasively explains that the proportion here relates the amount of exercise to the intensity of exercise. Thus, the 'composite acceleration correlates to both the amount and intensity of the activity.'

elements.label: [1.E]
elements.text: a display, coupled to the processing circuitry, to output the data which is representative of the activity points to the user.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Kroll ¶87 ('The device's display can present information such as total expended calories and activity intensities...') and the figures showing a CPU coupled to a display, and concluded Kroll discloses the claimed display that outputs data representative of activity points.

elements.text: a porous substrate
elements.board_determination_text: Institution was denied. The Board's references to Nishikawa teaching a microporous membrane (Ex. 1005, 2:47–51) appear in a background summary of the prior art, not as a formal finding of disclosure. No institution-level determination was made for this element.

elements.text: a porous layer arranged on a surface of the porous substrate
elements.board_determination_text: Institution was denied. The Board's description of Nishikawa disclosing a porous layer laminated on a microporous membrane (Ex. 1005, 2:47–51) is background context, not a formal finding of disclosure. No institution-level determination was made for this element.

elements.text: the porous layer comprises inorganic particles and a binder
elements.board_determination_text: Institution was denied. The Board's note that Nishikawa teaches a heat resistant porous layer containing an inorganic filler and resin (Ex. 1005, 2:47–51) is background context, not a formal finding of disclosure. No institution-level determination was made for this element.

elements.text: a ratio of Dv90 of the inorganic particles to the thickness of the porous layer is in a range from 0.3 to 3.0
elements.board_determination_text: The Board concluded that Petitioner's assumptions about (1) converting Nishikawa's weight‑based d90 to the patent's volume‑based Dv90 and (2) that Examples 4 and 5 have the same porous layer thickness as Example 1 "is not plainly supported by the record for purposes of an anticipation challenge which requires that a claimed property necessarily be present. Consequently, Petitioner does not present a compelling, meritorious challenge to claim 1 as anticipated by Nishikawa." (III.F.2).

elements.label: [1.PRE]
elements.text: A network access control system, comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: The Decision does not separately analyze the claim preamble; the Board's analysis addresses the claim limitations that follow.

elements.label: [1.A]
elements.text: an authentication module stored in memory and executable by a processor to authenticate a device seeking network access based at least on authentication information received from the device;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board does not separately determine whether Krantz discloses the 'authentication module' limitation. The Board mentions Krantz's teachings on passwords, EAP messages, certificates, keys, and cipher suites only in the context of analyzing the gatekeeper's 'formulate an audit request' limitation, concluding that those teachings relate to authentication rather than auditing. The Board's entire disposition turns on the 'formulate an audit request' deficiency and does not include an independent finding on this element.

elements.label: [1.B]
elements.text: a gatekeeper stored in memory and executable to: formulate an audit request, send the audit request to the device, evaluate device information received from the device in response to the audit request, and approve the device based on evaluation of the device information;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board's disposition turns on 'formulate an audit request' and concludes Petitioner has not shown that Krantz (or Krantz combined with Herrmann) teaches a gatekeeper that 'formulate[s] an audit request.' Because this sub-limitation was not shown, the Board determined Petitioner has not shown a reasonable likelihood to prevail on the claim as a whole.

elements.label: [1.B.i]
elements.text: formulate an audit request,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board explicitly found that 'Petitioner has not shown sufficiently that Krantz teaches or suggests "a gatekeeper . . . to: formulate an audit request."' The Board reasoned that Petitioner's cited teachings (passwords, EAP messages, certificates, keys, cipher suites) all relate to user authentication, not auditing, and that Petitioner 'fails to develop a coherent unpatentability theory with respect to both the "audit" and "authentication" limitations.' With respect to the Krantz-Herrmann combination, the Board found that Herrmann's RADIUS Access-Accept package is likewise 'directed to authentication and is not an audit request.'

elements.label: [1.B.ii]
elements.text: send the audit request to the device,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board's analysis focused on the 'formulate an audit request' limitation and did not separately resolve whether Krantz (or the combination) teaches 'send the audit request to the device' apart from the deficiency found for 'formulate.'

elements.label: [1.B.iii]
elements.text: evaluate device information received from the device in response to the audit request,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board's decision centers on the absence of a shown 'formulate an audit request' teaching; it does not separately find whether Krantz or the combination teaches 'evaluate device information received ... in response to the audit request.'

elements.label: [1.B.iv]
elements.text: approve the device based on evaluation of the device information;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately resolve whether Krantz or the combination teaches 'approve the device based on evaluation of the device information' beyond its finding that Petitioner failed to show the critical 'formulate an audit request' limitation.

elements.label: [1.C]
elements.text: an extensible authentication protocol (EAP) server layer (SL) stored in memory and executable to: receive the authentication information from the device using an EAP encryption protocol, and configure an access point in response to approval of the device by the gatekeeper.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board does not separately analyze whether Krantz or the combination teaches this EAP server layer limitation as a whole. The Board's disposition rests entirely on the 'formulate an audit request' deficiency. The dissent notes that Patent Owner did not even argue that the combination fails to meet this limitation (Dissent at 5 n.5), but the majority did not reach any independent determination on it.

elements.label: [1.C.i]
elements.text: receive the authentication information from the device using an EAP encryption protocol,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board does not make an independent finding on this sub-limitation. The Board mentions Krantz's EAP message exchanges (e.g., 'client 205 and server 215 exchange EAP-messages to pass certificates, keys, and supported cipher suites') only in the context of analyzing whether those teachings constitute 'formulating an audit request,' concluding they relate to authentication. The Board did not separately determine whether EAP-based receipt of authentication information is disclosed.

elements.label: [1.C.ii]
elements.text: configure an access point in response to approval of the device by the gatekeeper.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board does not separately analyze whether Krantz or the combination teaches configuring an access point in response to gatekeeper approval. Because the Board's disposition turns entirely on the 'formulate an audit request' deficiency, it did not reach this limitation. The dissent discusses Patent Owner's arguments on this point but the majority made no determination.

elements.label: [1PRE 1]
elements.text: A method for determining a common bit-loading modulation scheme for communicating between a plurality of nodes in a broadband cable network ("BCN"), the method comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner relied on Gurantz to teach 'the use of a bit-loading modulation scheme to communicate between a plurality of nodes within a broadband cable network (BCN),' which satisfies the BCN environment recited in the preamble. However, Petitioner acknowledged that 'Gurantz does not disclose that its determined bit-loading modulation scheme is a "common" scheme,' meaning the preamble's core characterization of determining a common scheme is absent from Gurantz alone.

elements.label: [1A]
elements.text: transmitting a probe signal from a transmitting node within the plurality of nodes to a sub-plurality of receiving nodes within the plurality of nodes;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner explicitly acknowledged that 'Gurantz does not disclose . . . transmitting a probe signal from the transmitting node to the sub-plurality of receiving nodes in connection with carrying out its bit-loading.' Petitioner relied on Grube for this element. The Board denied institution because Petitioner failed to show sufficient motivation to combine Gurantz and Grube.

elements.label: [1B]
elements.text: receiving a plurality of response signals from the sub-plurality of receiving nodes wherein each response signal includes a bit-loading modulation scheme determined by a corresponding receiving node;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner acknowledged that 'Gurantz . . . does not specifically describe [a bit-loading modulation] scheme being determined by a corresponding receiving node and included in a plurality of received response signals (responsive to the transmitted probe signal).' Petitioner relied on Grube for this element. The Board accepted Grube's disclosure of this element only arguendo in footnote 4, without making any finding about Gurantz.

elements.label: [1C]
elements.text: determining the common bit-loading modulation scheme from the received plurality of response signals;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner acknowledged that 'Gurantz . . . does not detail [a bit-loading modulation] scheme being determined by a corresponding receiving node and included in a plurality of received response signals (responsive to the transmitted probe signal), and determining a common bit-loading modulation scheme as recited.' Petitioner relied on Grube for this element.

elements.label: [1D]
elements.text: receiving the probe signal at one receiving node of the plurality of receiving nodes through a channel path of transmission;
elements.board_determination_text: Gurantz discloses no probe signal paradigm. Because Petitioner admitted Gurantz does not disclose transmitting a probe signal ([1A]), the receiving-node side of that same protocol is equally absent. Petitioner relied on Grube's secondary-site behavior (receiving a training signal) for this element. The Board found Petitioner had not shown sufficient motivation to combine Gurantz and Grube in the manner claimed.

elements.label: [1E]
elements.text: determining the transmission characteristics of the channel path at the one receiving node;
elements.board_determination_text: Determining transmission characteristics at a receiving node in response to a received probe signal is part of the probe/response protocol absent from Gurantz. Petitioner relied on Grube's secondary-site behavior of determining a bit-loading scheme responsive to a training signal for this element. The Board denied institution for failure to show sufficient motivation to combine Gurantz and Grube.

elements.label: [1F]
elements.text: transmitting a response signal from the one receiving node to the transmitting node,
elements.board_determination_text: Transmitting a response signal from a receiving node back to the transmitting node is part of the probe/response protocol that Petitioner admitted is absent from Gurantz. Petitioner acknowledged Gurantz 'does not specifically describe [a bit-loading modulation] scheme being determined by a corresponding receiving node and included in a plurality of received response signals (responsive to the transmitted probe signal),' which encompasses this response-signal transmission step.

elements.label: [1G]
elements.text: wherein the transmission characteristics of the channel path are determined by measuring the signal-to-noise ("SNR") characteristics of the received probe signal at the one receiving node
elements.board_determination_text: SNR measurement of a received probe signal at a receiving node is a sub-element of the probe/response protocol that Petitioner admitted is absent from Gurantz. Since Gurantz discloses no probe signal transmission or reception, measuring the SNR of such a signal is also absent. Petitioner relied on Grube's training-signal methodology for this element.

elements.label: [1H]
elements.text: wherein determining a common bit-loading modulation scheme includes: comparing a plurality of bit-loading modulation schemes from the corresponding received plurality of response signals; and determining the common bit-loading modulation scheme in response to comparing the plurality of bit-loaded modulation schemes.
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner acknowledged that 'Gurantz . . . does not detail [a bit-loading modulation] scheme being determined by a corresponding receiving node and included in a plurality of received response signals (responsive to the transmitted probe signal), and determining a common bit-loading modulation scheme as recited.' The Board accepted Grube's teaching of comparing bit-loading schemes to arrive at a common (LCD) scheme only arguendo in footnote 4, expressly stating it was 'accept[ing] these contentions . . . without making a determination about whether the evidence of record supports such a finding.'

elements.text: A system for capturing images and geo-location data corresponding thereto, comprising:

elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make an explicit determination for this element; the Decision observes the Petition lacks sufficient particularity in mapping prior art to many claim elements.

elements.label: 1[c]
elements.text: at least one geo-locating device, each said at least one geo-locating device issuing a corresponding at least one geo-locating signal, each said at least one geo-locating signal being indicative at least in part of a geo-location of said first image-capturing device during each first image capturing event;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not expressly determine whether this limitation is taught by the asserted prior art; the Decision addresses general insufficiency of the Petition rather than making a per-element finding for this limitation.

elements.label: 1[d]
elements.text: a computer system receiving and storing said first and second image-data signals and said at least one geo-locating signal;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make a substantive finding that the prior art fails to disclose [1.d]. The Board used [1.d] only as an illustrative example of the Petition's insufficient particularity and improper incorporation by reference, noting Petitioner's string cite parenthetical for [1.d] was less than one page while the cited portions of Dr. Forsyth's testimony comprised about 13 pages plus an additional nine pages in Appendix C. The dispositive finding was procedural (insufficient particularity), not a merits determination on disclosure.

elements.label: 1[e]
elements.text: the computer system executing image and data acquiring software reading said first and second image-data signals and said at least one geo-locating signal, said image and data acquiring software associating each said first or second image-data signal with a corresponding said at least one geo-locating signal for each image-capturing event;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not make an independent substantive determination about whether [1.e] is disclosed. Patent Owner noted that [1.e] was the only limitation for which the Petition provided any explanatory mapping (the 'coordinating' and 'labeling' in Loewen to the 'associating' of [1.e]), and cited this as evidence of the Petition's overall deficiency for all other elements. The Board agreed with Patent Owner's general argument about insufficient particularity but did not separately analyze [1.e].

elements.board_determination_text: Consequently, we determine that Petitioner has failed to sufficiently show that the combination of Loewen and Fujimoto teaches element [1.f], and thus has failed to establish a reasonable likelihood that claim 1 is unpatentable over this combination.

elements.label: [pre]
elements.text: A power converter coupled to a power system controller configured to receive a signal indicating a system operational state of a load coupled thereto
elements.board_determination_text: Based on this finding and the findings in sections II.D.2.a and b.1 above, we find that Chagny describes the subject matter recited in the preamble of claim 1. (Section II.D.2.a)

elements.label: [a]
elements.text: a power switch configured to conduct for a duty cycle to provide a regulated output characteristic at an output thereof
elements.board_determination_text: We find that Chagny describes a duty cycle because Chagny discloses that charge switch 220 is turned on and off, which results in a conduction period, non-conduction period, and a switching period. ... Thus, Chagny describes that power switch 220 has a duty cycle. (Section II.D.2.a)

elements.label: [b]
elements.text: a controller configured to receive a command from said power system controller to enter a power converter operational state as a function of said signal indicating said system operational state
elements.board_determination_text: Thus, we find that Chagny’s disclosure of controller module 210 receiving activity input 202 and selecting different power converter switching frequencies based on that input describes 'a controller configured to receive a command from said power system controller to enter a power converter operational state as a function of said signal indicating said system operational state.' (Section II.D.2.b.3)

elements.label: [c]
elements.text: said controller further configured to provide a signal to control said duty cycle of said power switch as a function of said output characteristic and in accordance with said command
elements.board_determination_text: Based on Petitioner’s contentions and evidence and for the reasons discussed above, we find that Chagny discloses controlling the duty cycle of charge switch 220 as a function of the feedback from the regulated DC voltage output 295 ... We also find that Chagny discloses controlling the duty cycle of charge switch 220 in accordance with the command to enter a power converter operational state based on Chagny’s disclosure that frequency selector module 215 receives activity input 202 and selects switching frequency 216 ... (Section II.D.2.b.2)

elements.label: [d]

elements.label: [10.P]
elements.text: A plate for securing two discrete bones together across an intermediate joint, comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner argued that 'if claim 10's preamble is limiting, Slater [discloses] a plate for securing two discrete bones together across an intermediate joint,' citing Slater's Figure 1 as showing a plate secured to two bones (tibia and talus) across the ankle joint. The Board did not dispute this characterization and focused its denial on element [10.4].

elements.text: an elongate spine having: a first end comprising: at least one fixation point for attaching the first end to a first discrete bone on a first side of a joint; and a first inner surface configured to substantially conform with a geometry of the first bone;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner contended that 'Slater discloses claim 10's elongate spine and first and second ends, as well as a bridge portion between the ends,' citing Slater's Figure 1 and features depicted therein (Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 109–114). The Board did not challenge this characterization; its denial of claim 10 was based solely on element [10.4].

elements.text: a second end comprising: at least one fixation point for attaching the second end to a second discrete bone on a second side of the joint; and a second inner surface configured to substantially conform with a geometry of the second bone; and
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner contended that Slater discloses claim 10's first and second ends as well as a bridge portion, citing Slater's Figure 1 and features depicted therein (Pet. 21–26; Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 109–114). The Board did not separately challenge this characterization; its denial was based solely on element [10.4].

elements.text: a bridge portion disposed between the first end and the second end; and
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner contended that Slater discloses a bridge portion between the ends, citing Slater's Figure 1 (Pet. 21–26; Ex. 1002 ¶¶ 109–114). The Board did not challenge this characterization and noted in its analysis that Petitioner's annotation of Figure 1 identified the bridge portion (yellow arrow and oval).

elements.text: a transfixation screw hole disposed along the spine, the transfixation screw hole comprising an inner surface configured to direct a transfixation screw through the transfixation screw hole such that the transfixation screw extends alongside the bridge portion at a trajectory configured to pass through a first position on the first bone and a second position on the second bone, enabling said screw to absorb tensile load when the second bone is loaded permitting transfer of the tensile load through said screw into said bridge,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board found: 'we find that Petitioner has not established that Slater expressly or inherently discloses the transfer of tensile load limitations in claims 10 and 16, and, therefore does not prove, by a preponderance of the evidence, that Slater discloses each element of either of claim 10 or 16.' The concurring opinion additionally found that 'Slater does not disclose the transfixation screw hole comprising an inner surface configured to direct [a] transfixation screw . . . at a trajectory' because 'Slater's opening 26 is meant to be a variable angle hole and not an opening configured to direct a screw at a particular angle or trajectory.'

elements.text: wherein at least a portion of said bridge portion and said transfixation screw hole has a depth greater than at least a portion of said first and second ends.
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner contended that 'Slater uses the terms depth and thickness interchangeably and otherwise discloses limitation 10.5,' citing Slater's disclosure that the plate should have 'maximum thickness' at the region where highest loading will occur in normal use (Pet. 29–31; Ex. 1005, 15:19–23). The Board did not challenge this characterization; its denial was based solely on element [10.4].

elements.label: (a)(i)
elements.text: (a) a crRNA segment comprising (i) a guide sequence capable of hybridizing to a target sequence in a polynucleotide
elements.board_determination_text: Table 8 in Pioneer Hi-Bred discloses exemplary crRNA molecules comprising 2'-O-methyl modifications in the VT domain (e.g., sequences 66 and 68). The VT domain corresponds to the "guide sequence" in claims 1 and 19 because it is "complementary to a nucleotide sequence in a target DNA" meaning that it hybridizes to a target sequence in that polynucleotide.

elements.label: (a)(ii)
elements.text: (a) a crRNA segment comprising (ii) a stem sequence
elements.board_determination_text: Pioneer Hi-Bred teaches that crRNAs, including those in Table 8, also have a CER domain (i.e., a stem sequence) that is complementary to and paired with a tracrRNA to form a duplex guide RNA.

elements.label: (b)
elements.text: (b) a tracrRNA segment comprising a nucleotide sequence that is partially or completely complementary to the stem sequence
elements.board_determination_text: Pioneer Hi-Bred teaches that crRNAs, including those in Table 8, also have a CER domain (i.e., a stem sequence) that is complementary to and paired with a tracrRNA to form a duplex guide RNA.

elements.label: gRNA functionality
elements.text: wherein the synthetic guide RNA has gRNA functionality comprising associating with a Cas protein and targeting the gRNA:Cas protein complex to the target sequence
elements.board_determination_text: Pioneer Hi-Bred defines the term "guide polynucleotide" as "a polynucleotide sequence that can form a complex with a Cas endonuclease and enables the Cas endonuclease to recognize and optionally cleave a DNA target site." Thus, Pioneer Hi-Bred discloses that the guide polynucleotides described therein can: (1) form a complex with a Cas endonuclease; and (2) enable the endonuclease to recognize a DNA target site. That disclosure reads on both the associating and targeting aspects of the "gRNA functionality" recited in claims 1 and 19.

elements.label: 2'-O-methyl modification
elements.text: and comprises one or more modifications in the guide sequence wherein the one or more modifications comprises a 2'-O-methyl
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner has shown that Pioneer Hi-Bred discloses guide RNA and crRNA molecules having 2'-O-methyl modifications as recited in claims 1 and 19. In particular, Table 8 in Pioneer Hi-Bred discloses exemplary crRNA molecules comprising 2'-O-methyl modifications in the VT domain (e.g., sequences 66 and 68).

In particular, we are persuaded that Kroll's disclosure of a tri-axial accelerometer is sufficient to disclose the recited three accelerometers, and Kroll also discloses the use of multiple tri-axial accelerometers.

elements.label: [1.C]
elements.text: processing circuitry, disposed in the housing and electrically coupled to the plurality of sensors, to: calculate the activity points of the user using the sensor data, wherein the activity points correlate to an amount of one or more physical activities of the user;
elements.board_determination_text: Petitioner relies upon Kroll's CPU to disclose the recited 'processing circuitry.' As discussed above, we are persuaded that Kroll provides a singular teaching as to the CPU and as such there is no improper mixing of disclosures from differing embodiments.

elements.label: [1.D]
elements.text: a display, coupled to the processing circuitry, to output the data which is representative of the activity points to the user.
elements.board_determination_text: As to the display, Petitioner directs us to paragraph 87, which states '[t]he device's display can present information such as total expended calories and activity intensities alphanumerically, graphically, or both. Progress toward a daily activity or calorie burn goal can be presented to the user on such a display or simple indicator lights.' In addition, each of Figures 7–10 depicts a CPU coupled to a display.

elements.label: [1.PREAMBLE]
elements.text: A portable activity monitoring device to calculate activity points corresponding to physical activities of a user, the portable activity monitoring device comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: For all of the foregoing reasons, we are persuaded that Petitioner has established by a preponderance of the evidence that claims 1, 10, and 25 are unpatentable under § 102 as anticipated by Kroll.

elements.label: [1.A]
elements.text: a housing having a physical size and shape that is adapted to couple to the body of the user;
elements.board_determination_text: Kroll's Figure 7 shows the body worn device and the Board notes that 'each of the components is contained within a portable housing.' (Ex. 1004 ¶¶ 27, 85).

elements.label: [1.B]
elements.text: a plurality of sensors, disposed in the housing, to generate sensor data which is representative of activity of the user, wherein the plurality of sensors includes at least three accelerometers;
elements.board_determination_text: For all of the foregoing reasons, we determine that Kroll discloses this limitation (the Board found Kroll's disclosure of tri-axial accelerometers sufficient to disclose the recited three accelerometers and that the claimed 'plurality of sensors' is met).

elements.label: [1.C]
elements.text: processing circuitry, disposed in the housing and electrically coupled to the plurality of sensors, to: calculate the activity points of the user using the sensor data, wherein the activity points correlate to an amount of one or more physical activities of the user;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board was 'persuaded that Kroll provides a singular teaching as to the CPU' which Petitioner relies on for the recited 'processing circuitry,' and further concluded that Kroll 'calculates and displays "total expended calories and activity intensities,"' with the 'composite acceleration' being proportional to calorie expenditure and correlating to amount (and intensity) of activity.

elements.label: [1.D]
elements.text: a display, coupled to the processing circuitry, to output the data which is representative of the activity points to the user.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board cited Kroll's disclosure that 'The device's display can present information such as total expended calories and activity intensities alphanumerically, graphically, or both.' (Ex. 1004 ¶ 87) and found that Kroll discloses a display coupled to the CPU that outputs activity-related data.

elements.label: a)
elements.text: A method for providing distributed access services between a watcher and at least one personal presentity, the method comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: Based on the entirety of the record and for the reasons explained by Petitioner, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that, to the extent the preamble is limiting, Oh teaches the preamble of claim 1.

elements.label: b)
elements.text: receiving, at a computing device, an alert from a personal presentity;
elements.board_determination_text: Based on the entirety of the record and for the reasons explained by Petitioner, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that Oh teaches this limitation.

elements.label: c)
elements.text: determining a current location of the watcher;
elements.board_determination_text: Based on the entirety of the record, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that Oh teaches this claim limitation.

elements.label: d)
elements.text: providing a notification associated with the alert to the watcher; and
elements.board_determination_text: Based on the entirety of the record and for the reasons explained by Petitioner, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that Oh teaches this limitation.

elements.label: e)
elements.text: storing data related to a current location of the watcher.
elements.board_determination_text: Based on the entirety of the record, we determine that Petitioner has proved by a preponderance of the evidence that Oh teaches this claim limitation.

elements.label: [pre]
elements.text: A communication terminal, comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: The Board did not separately analyze the preamble and did not construe any claim terms ('Accordingly, we do not construe any claim terms expressly'). Petitioner's characterization of Coffee's MDT as a communication terminal was uncontested, so the preamble is treated as disclosed.

elements.label: [1a]
elements.text: a receiver for receiving a formatted message out of a number of pre-defined formatted messages, comprising a message identification code and a plurality of information fields;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Coffee's disclosure of a 'Received Message Data' packet (7102) that contains either the full text or an ID number and that includes IOD and response information (discussing Coffee's Table 58 and related text).

elements.label: [1b]
elements.text: an output device for presenting said formatted message to a user of said communication terminal;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Coffee's description of the MDT as a display device (LCD) that enables the vehicle operator to view and respond to text paging messages (citing Coffee's MDT description).

elements.label: [1c]
elements.text: an input device for responding to said formatted message;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Coffee's disclosure that the MDT has a keypad/softkeys and that the operator selects responses using arrow keys/softkeys (citing Coffee's discussion of selecting responses and the MDT keypad).

elements.label: [1d]
elements.text: a memory for storing said formatted message and for storing field-mapping information, said field-mapping information for indicating information to be copied from said formatted message to a response message based on which one of said pre-defined formatted messages was received;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board acknowledged that Coffee discloses an MDT with memory that stores received messages, but did not credit Petitioner's showing that Coffee discloses or inherently stores the claimed 'field-mapping information'—the Board focused its analysis on the 'copied/filled in' limitations and found Petitioner's evidence for field-mapping was not established on the record.

elements.label: [1e1]
elements.text: a processor for creating said response message in response to an indication from said input device of a request to respond to said formatted message,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Coffee's teaching that the MDT/tracker processing circuitry handles message requests and responses (Coffee describes MDT/tracker communication and response generation), supporting that a processor creates a response message.

elements.label: [1e2]
elements.text: said response message comprising at least one response message information field,
elements.board_determination_text: The Board cited Coffee's Table 63 (Text Message Response, 7202) showing the response message contains fields such as the IOD and a numeric response value, i.e., response message information fields.

elements.label: [1e3]
elements.text: wherein information from at least one of said plurality of information fields of said formatted message is copied into at least one of said response message information fields based on said field-mapping information;
elements.board_determination_text: The Board expressly found that 'Petitioner fails to show, by a preponderance of the evidence, that Coffee discloses that information or information fields are "copied" or "filled in" from a formatted message to a response message' and that Petitioner did not establish that Coffee explicitly or inherently discloses such copying (including copying of the IOD, header, or response fields). This was the dispositive element causing the Board to find the challenged claims not unpatentable.

elements.label: [1f]
elements.text: a transmitter for transmitting said information relating to said response message.
elements.board_determination_text: The Board relied on Coffee's disclosure that the MDT sends the selected response back to the tracker (e.g., Text Message Response 7202), showing transmission of response-message information.

elements.board_determination_text: We determine that Chen inherently teaches a formulation that 'electrostatically attracts the particulate matter to the thin film,' the 'CATCH' element.

elements.board_determination_text: Having considered the parties’ positions and evidence of record, we determine that Chen discloses a formulation having the 'HOLD' element of claim 2. elements.label: 1c

elements.label: [1pre]
elements.text: A remote wakeup method, comprising:
elements.board_determination_text: "We agree and find that Zhang discloses a remote wakeup method, even if the preamble is limiting."

elements.label: [1a]
elements.text: a connection server receiving a wakeup request packet from a first apparatus,
elements.board_determination_text: "We find that Zhang’s query request (Fig. 3, step 306) sent by end user 406 ("a first apparatus") to server 404 ("a connection server") is a 'wakeup request packet,' and that Zhang discloses claim limitation 1a."

elements.label: [1a.i]
elements.text: wherein the wakeup request packet comprises a device identifier of a second apparatus,
elements.board_determination_text: "We find this disclosed by Zhang’s description of the query request including identification information for sleeping client 408, including a user-selected human-readable ID of the sleeping client."

elements.label: [1a.ii]
elements.text: wherein the first apparatus is connected to the connection server via local area network or internet;
elements.board_determination_text: "Zhang’s WAN shows that end user 406 is connected to server 404 via a local area network or Internet."

elements.label: [1b]
elements.text: the connection server obtaining wakeup information stored in the connection server according to the wakeup request packet,
elements.board_determination_text: "We find this disclosed by Zhang’s description of Zhang’s query reply from server 404 to end user 406 with information, including a nonce stored in the server’s memory, needed by end user 406 to generate and send a wakeup message to sleeping client 408."

elements.label: [1b.i]
elements.text: wherein the wakeup information stored in the connection server is the same as wakeup information stored in the second apparatus;
elements.board_determination_text: "We find this disclosed by Zhang’s description of sleeping client 408 storing the same nonce."

elements.label: [1c]
elements.text: the connection server generating an apparatus wakeup packet according to the wakeup request packet and the wakeup information,
elements.board_determination_text: "As this packet is generated by server 404 according to the wakeup request packet and the wakeup information, we find that Zhang discloses claim limitation 1c."

elements.label: [1c.i]
elements.text: wherein the apparatus wakeup packet comprises the device identifier of the second apparatus from the wakeup request packet and the obtained wakeup information;
elements.board_determination_text: "We find this disclosed by Zhang’s description of including the user-selected human-readable ID of sleeping client 408 and the nonce in the apparatus wakeup packet."

elements.label: [1d]
elements.text: the connection server obtaining a network address corresponding to the second apparatus from a storage unit of the connection server according to the wakeup request packet;
elements.board_determination_text: "We find that Zhang discloses server 404 obtaining a network address of sleeping client 408, and that the network address is registered and stored by sleeping client 408 in the server’s memory and associated with the device identifier."

elements.label: [1e]
elements.text: the connection server sending the apparatus wakeup packet to the second apparatus based on the network address,
elements.board_determination_text: "We find that Zhang’s server 404 sends the wakeup packet to sleeping client 408 at the network address retrieved from the server’s memory."

elements.label: [1e.i]
elements.text: wherein wakeup feature data is a segment of data of the apparatus wakeup packet and is for determining whether the entire second apparatus be awoken.
elements.board_determination_text: "We find that Zhang’s nonce is a segment of data qualifying as 'wakeup feature data,' and that the nonce is used to determine whether to wake the entire sleeping client 408."
